title: Intervention with Screening in Panic-Based Runs
abstract: Policymakers frequently use guarantees to mitigate panic-based runs in the financial system. We analyze a binary-action coordination game under the global games framework and propose a novel intervention program that screens investors based on their heterogeneous beliefs about the system's stability. The program only attracts investors who are at the margin of running, and their participation boosts all investors' confidence in the financial system. Compared with government guarantee programs, our proposed program is as effective at mitigating runs but features two advantages: it costs less to implement and it is robust to moral hazard.

article.introduction:
In the modern financial system, coordination failures among investors and financial institutions can lead to self-fulfilling, panic-based ‚Äúruns‚ÄĚ that threaten the system's stability and generate huge welfare losses.1Policymakers worldwide have therefore devoted considerable effort and resources to intervention programs that aim to prevent panic among investors. For instance, during the Great Recession of 2007 to 2008, governments provided financial institutions with loans, explicit and implicit guarantees, and capital injections. While these policies proved to be effective at restoring financial stability, they were criticized on two grounds. First, implementing guarantee programs on such a large scale entailed huge fiscal costs that jeopardized the sustainability of sovereign debt and led to a sovereign debt crisis in several European countries (Acharya, Drechsler, and Schnabl (2014), Farhi and Tirole (2018)). Second, the policies were criticized for their vulnerability to moral hazard problems (Cooper and Ross (2002), Allen et¬†al. (2018)).
  Given the downside of conventional interventions, a natural question to ask is whether the drawbacks can be overcome without reducing the effectiveness of measures intended to prevent self-fulfilling runs. We address this question by analyzing a coordination game in the context of bank runs in a standard global games framework (Morris and Shin (2003)). In such an environment, even under minimal information frictions, bank investors hold diverse beliefs about the likelihood of bank failure. We therefore propose a type of subsidy‚Äďtax program with voluntary participation that screens agents based on their beliefs. Compared with conventional programs such as government guarantees, our program offers two advantages. First, only a small group of pivotal investors‚ÄĒthose matter most for the coordination outcome‚ÄĒself-select into the program, thereby saving implementation costs while achieving the same effectiveness. Second, the disciplinary role of bank runs is preserved, which reduces moral hazard of¬†banks.
  In the benchmark model, a continuum of investors simultaneously decide whether to run on a bank. A¬†key aspect of this model is that run decisions feature strategic complementarities. Since a bank fails if either its fundamentals are weak or a critical mass of investors run on the bank, an investor has more incentive to run if he expects others to do likewise. As in standard global games, each investor receives a noisy private signal about the bank's fundamentals and makes inferences about other investors' run decisions. In the unique equilibrium, investors follow a threshold strategy: they run if their private signals fall below a certain signal threshold. As a result, the bank defaults if its fundamentals fall below a fundamental threshold. In a region of fundamentals just below that threshold, the bank's failure is self-fulfilling: it fails because of coordination failures among investors, whereas it would have survived if investors had not run on the bank. Thus, to improve social welfare, a policymaker seeks to minimize self-fulfilling bank runs by reducing the fundamental¬†threshold.
  Accordingly, we allow the policymaker to offer all investors a subsidy‚Äďtax program with voluntary participation. An investor who accepts the offer and does not run on the bank will receive a subsidy if the bank fails and will pay a tax if the bank survives. We classify responses to this intervention into three categories based on the tax-to-subsidy ratio. If a program has a high tax-to-subsidy ratio, then no investor will participate, and hence, the program has no effect on the run threshold. We refer to this program as azero-participation program(ZPP). If a program charges a nonpositive tax, then participating investors will always receive nonnegative transfers from the policymaker, and hence, the program attracts all investors. We call this program afull-participation program(FPP). Many existing intervention policies, including government guarantees, benefit all investors uniformly and thus fall into this category. In this paper, we proposepartial-participation programs(PPPs), which feature positive but low tax-to-subsidy ratios such that some but not all investors participate. Conceptually, a PPP is similar to a costly insurance policy that charges a premium if the bank survives and provides protection if it fails. More importantly, PPPs screen investors based on their beliefs about the probability of bank¬†failure.
  Under a PPP, the most optimistic investors‚ÄĒthose that view the bank's failure as unlikely‚ÄĒstay in the bank and decline to participate in the program because they consider the insurance to be too expensive. In contrast, the most pessimistic investors believe that the probability of bank failure is so high that staying in the bank is not an attractive option, even if they are protected by the insurance. Therefore, only investors with intermediate beliefs will participate in the program. From their perspective, the insurance is reasonably priced and staying in the bank is attractive given the protection offered by the insurance. We show that under PPP, there is a unique equilibrium in which all investors follow a threshold strategy with two thresholds. An investor will stay in the bank without participating in the program if he receives a high signal, will stay in the bank and participate in the program if his signal is intermediate (i.e., between the two thresholds), and will run if he receives a low¬†signal.
  Although FPPs and PPPs can both reduce coordination failures, we derive the powerful result that any PPP incurs a lower expected cost of implementation than does any FPP that achieves the same coordination outcome.2This result is crucial for a policymaker who has a limited budget and must allocate her funding economically among several welfare-enhancing programs. Moreover, the cost savings of a PPP relative to an FPP arise from both the extensive and the intensive margins. On the extensive margin, a PPP excludes the most optimistic investors, so the policymaker subsidizes fewer investors than under an¬†FPP. On the intensive margin, a PPP collects taxes from participants when the bank survives, which partially offsets the cost of providing subsidies in¬†expectation.
  To understand how PPPs reduce coordination failure at minimal cost, it is useful to go through the following thought process. Start with the original threshold equilibrium without an intervention program. In this equilibrium, investors with signals slightly below the run threshold would run on the bank. A¬†PPP provides these investors with protection against bank failure and incentivizes them to stay in the bank. Anticipating that the introduction of a PPP will incentivize more investors to stay in the bank, all investors have greater confidence in the bank's survival. This persuades investors with even lower signals to accept the PPP offer and stay, which is again anticipated by investors and further strengthens their incentive to stay in the bank. Repeating this thought process, the PPP-induced incentive to stay is amplified by higher order beliefs, so coordination failures can be substantially reduced in equilibrium. At the same time, as investors are more optimistic about the bank's survival, the downside protection offered by the PPP becomes less appealing, and thus, in equilibrium, the mass of investors who accept the offer¬†is¬†small.
  In addition to the advantage of lower implementation costs, PPPs are also more robust (than FPPs) to moral hazard problems. We establish this robustness by incorporating moral hazard (see Diamond and Rajan (2001)) into the model and then comparing government guarantee programs‚ÄĒthe least costly FPP with zero tax and a positive subsidy‚ÄĒwith PPPs. More specifically, the banker is allowed to choose an effort level after the policymaker announces the intervention program but before investors make their run decisions. The banker enjoys a private benefit from shirking, but shirking reduces investors' payoffs from staying in the bank and so increases the likelihood of a bank run. Since the banker is rewarded if the bank survives, bank runs play a disciplinary role by increasing the cost of shirking. So, in the absence of an intervention program, the disciplinary effect of bank runs encourages the banker to exert welfare-maximizing¬†effort.
  Government guarantee programs reduce an investor's ‚Äúskin in the game‚ÄĚ and make his run decision insensitive to the banker's effort choice. Government guarantee programs therefore impede the monitoring role of bank runs and fail to limit shirking by the banker. In contrast, a PPP can achieve the same coordination outcome while preserving the monitoring role of bank runs. The reason is that investors internalize the cost of participating in a PPP. If the banker shirks, which reduces investors' payoffs from staying in the bank, then the tax charged by the PPP becomes so costly that no investor will participate in the program. The PPP therefore serves as a switching mechanism: it deters bank runs if the banker exerts effort, but it turns into a ZPP (and hence becomes inoperative) if the banker shirks. Accordingly, PPPs can reduce coordination failure without inducing moral¬†hazard.
  Our proposed PPP has a wide range of applications beyond bank runs; examples include credit market freezes (Bebchuk and Goldstein (2011)) and asset market freezes (Bernardo and Welch (2004)). In some of these applications, moral hazard arises not at the aggregate level but rather at the individual level. To illustrate, consider our model in the context of a credit freeze, where banks‚ÄĒinstead of investors‚ÄĒare the strategic players. In a panic-based credit freeze, banks abstain from lending in anticipation of low aggregate credit supply and low profits from lending. Government guarantees encourage bank lending but reduce banks' skin in the game and thus their incentive to screen and monitor borrowers. Unlike the moral hazard in our benchmark model, a bank's shirking in credit assessment and monitoring reduces its own profits from lending but does not directly spill over to other banks. In other words, the moral hazard problem occurs at the individual level. To demonstrate the robustness of PPPs to individual moral hazard, we modify the benchmark model and assume that a bank can derive a private benefit from shirking, which reduces the bank's profits from lending in the absence of a credit freeze. Since FPPs (e.g., government guarantees) and PPPs both subsidize banks' losses, they lead to shirking by participating banks. However, fewer banks participate in PPPs than in¬†FPPs. It is important to bear in mind that, for the most optimistic banks, the tax component of a PPP is so costly that they optimally decline the offer‚ÄĒwhich incentivizes them to exert effort. So, under PPPs, the individual moral hazard problem is limited to medium-belief banks, the mass of which approaches zero in the limit of vanishing information frictions. As a result, in the limit, there exist PPPs that can restore the first-best outcome; in contrast, no government guarantee program can do so because of the welfare loss resulting from individual moral¬†hazard.
  The actual implementation of our proposed PPP can take various forms. Besides the direct interpretation of a policymaker offering an intervention program to all investors, we examine the viability of privatizing the intervention program. Two caveats are in order. First, since the provider of an intervention program must make large payments in the ‚Äúrun‚ÄĚ state, investors may worry about counterparty risk when participating in a private program. Second, privatization comes with information production and revelation, which may aggravate panic and undermine the effectiveness of the intervention¬†program.
  Literature. To the best of our knowledge, our proposed PPPs that screen investors based on their beliefs is novel to the literature. In particular, we demonstrate two main advantages of such programs: cost efficiency and robustness to moral hazard. Similar to our mechanism, several papers explore policies that target a specific group of agents to reduce coordination failure. For example, Sakovics and Steiner (2012) and Choi (2014) analyze coordination games with ex¬†ante heterogeneous agents and argue that the optimal subsidy schedule is to target a certain type of investors.3PPPs contrast with these targeted intervention programs in mainly three respects. First, we show that it is cost efficient to screen agents based on their interim beliefs rather than ex¬†ante characteristics. In other words, policymakers should target interim rather than ex¬†ante ‚Äúpivotal‚ÄĚ investors. Second, while implementing a targeted intervention program requires the policymaker to correctly identify each investor's type, which can entail information acquisition costs, PPPs incentivize ‚Äúpivotal‚ÄĚ investors to self-reveal their type, which allows the policymaker to offer a uniform intervention policy to all investors. Lastly, these papers focus on direct subsidies without a tax charge, which is a special type of the FPPs in our model, whereas the policy space that we consider is more general. We compare PPPs and these targeted intervention programs in more detail in AppendixA.
  Our paper also contributes to the vast literature on panic-based bank runs and policies to enhance financial stability dating back to Diamond and Dybvig (1983).4Although government guarantee programs such as deposit insurance are proposed as an effective intervention to reduce coordination failures among bank investors, they suffer limitations that have been pointed out in the literature. In particular, large-scale government guarantees link the stability of banks and sovereign states, which can lead bank runs to jeopardize the fiscal health of a country (Acharya, Drechsler, and Schnabl (2014), Leonello (2018), Farhi and Tirole (2018)), and such programs are vulnerable to moral hazard problems (Cooper and Ross (2002), Allen et¬†al. (2015,2018)).5Compared to government guarantees, our proposed program overcomes these drawbacks without compromising its effectiveness in preventing panic¬†runs.
  In terms of methodology, our model builds on the global games literature pioneered by Carlsson and Van¬†Damme (1993). Morris and Shin (2003) review the commonly applied setup and applications of global games. Our benchmark model in SectionIis a special case with binary payoffs; we generalize the payoff structure in AppendixB. Researchers have applied global games techniques to analyze coordination failures in various contexts such as bank runs (Rochet and Vives (2004), Goldstein and Pauzner (2005)), currency attacks (Morris and Shin (1998)), credit freezes (Bebchuk and Goldstein (2011)), debt rollovers (Morris and Shin (2004), He and Xiong (2012)), and financial market runs (Bernardo and Welch (2004), Brunnermeier and Pedersen (2009)). Although we focus on bank runs, our model has a wide range of applications. As long as the policymaker can observe agents' actions and condition the intervention program on those actions, implementing our proposed program can reduce coordination¬†failures.
  From a theoretical perspective, our study is most closely related to Morris and Shadmehr (2017), who analyze the reward schemes that a revolutionary leader can offer in order to elicit effort from citizens. The ideal reward scheme also screens citizens based on their beliefs. However, the authors consider mandatory reward schemes, whereas we focus on intervention programs with voluntary participation. A¬†more crucial distinction is that while Morris and Shadmehr (2017) assume that any reward scheme can be implemented at zero cost, we explicitly model and aim to minimize the cost of intervention. Our study is also related to Cong, Grenadier, and Hu (2020) and Basak and Zhou (2020), who explore intervention policies under dynamic settings. In both of these papers, the policymaker targets a subset of agents in each period; successful coordination in previous periods serves as a public signal that upholds agents' confidence in subsequent periods. Our paper, in contrast, considers a static coordination game in which investors' confidence is affected by their inferences about other investors' reactions to an intervention program, and hence, the effectiveness of our proposed program does not rely on a dynamic information structure. Another related paper is Bond and Hagerty (2010), who in a context of crime waves analyze the welfare implications of introducing a medium action of moderate crime with a mild penalty if detected. Our proposed PPPs are similar in the sense that they also introduce a medium action with moderate benefit and mild cost, but differ in that Bond and Hagerty (2010) focus on the distinct welfare implications arising under different equilibria, while we focus on the amplification effect of higher order beliefs when agents have asymmetric information about the state of the¬†world.
  The rest of our paper proceeds as follows. In SectionI, we present a benchmark model of bank runs and introduce intervention policies that can mitigate panic-based runs. SectionsIIandIIIcompare the proposed program with government guarantees in terms of implementation costs and robustness to moral hazard, respectively. SectionIVdiscusses issues related to the implementation of our proposed program. We conclude in SectionV.

article.II. Cost of Intervention:
In this section, we compare the welfare achieved by different intervention programs and show that PPPs dominate FPPs in two steps. In SectionII.A, we define the policymaker's welfare maximization problem. The key step in solving the policymaker's problem is to find a cost-minimizing intervention program that achieves a socially optimal fundamental threshold. In SectionII.B, we prove that achieving any target fundamental threshold always incurs lower implementation costs under a PPP than under an FPP. We then characterize the PPP with the lowest implementation¬†cost.

article.III. Moral Hazard:
Government guarantee programs are criticized for reducing moral hazard. In this section, we illustrate the second major advantage of PPPs, namely, robustness to moral hazard. Moral hazard takes various forms in different contexts. In SectionIII.A, we examine the aggregate moral hazard problem at the bank level in the context of bank runs. In SectionIII.B, we examine the individual moral hazard problem at the investor level in the context of a market¬†freeze.
  It is widely accepted that the threat of bank runs can discipline moral hazard problems in financial institutions (Calomiris and Kahn (1991), Diamond and Rajan (2001)). For example, banks may shirk in credit assessment or provide loans with high default risk, or fund managers may engage in excessive risk-taking because they do not bear the full cost in the case of downside realizations. Investors can discipline financial institutions by the threat of early withdrawal. If a bank's portfolio is impaired, investors are likely to withdraw their funds early, which increases the bank's incentive to maximize the performance of its investment portfolio. Although government guarantees (e.g., deposit insurance) deter panic-based runs, they reduce investors' skin in the game and therefore limit the disciplinary effect of runs. In this section, we show that PPPs are more efficient than FPPs‚ÄĒincluding government guarantee programs‚ÄĒin the presence of such moral¬†hazard.
  To incorporate moral hazard, we modify the benchmark model as follows. AtT=0$T=0$, after the policymaker announces the intervention program but before the realization of¬†őł, the bank decides whether (or not) to exert effort in screening and monitoring its long-term investments. However, if the bank shirks (ie=0$i^e=0$), the quality of its investment deteriorates, and the bank will experience a fundamental failure atT=2$T=2$with probabilityő≥>0$\gamma >0$even in the absence of a run. In the case of a fundamental failure, investors who stay in the bank recover nothing from their investment and receive a payoff of ‚ąí1, as in a bank run. Thus, in the absence of a run, the expected return to investors falls to(1‚ąíő≥)R$(1-\gamma )R$if the bank shirks, which gives them an incentive to monitor the bank. For simplicity, we assume that the bank's choice of effortie$i^e$is observable to investors so that they can monitor the bank and condition their run decisions on the bank's effort choice. In addition, we assume(1‚ąíő≥)R>1$(1-\gamma )R>1$, because otherwise shirking by the bank can easily induce a bank run, and government guarantee programs witht=0$t=0$will not create any moral hazard¬†problem.
  In SectionII, we show that if information frictions are nonnegligible (ŌÉ>0$\sigma >0$), then PPPs incur strictly lower costs than FPPs. We therefore focus on the case of negligible information frictions (ŌÉ‚Üí0$\sigma \rightarrow 0$).   Under an FPP, the policymaker faces a trade-off between coordination efficiency and effort efficiency. On the one hand, insuring investors against losses from bank runs incentivizes them to stay in the bank. On the other hand, that guarantee reduces their incentive to monitor the bank. If the policymaker aims to eliminate coordination failure, then she must tolerate the bank's resulting moral hazard problem. In contrast, a PPP not only deters bank runs but also prevents banks from shirking. The subsidy incentivizes investors to stay in the bank, while the tax restores their incentive to monitor the bank. So, if a bank shirks, the tax discourages investors from participating in the program, which eliminates its stabilizing effect and thereby increases the likelihood of bank runs. The threat of bank runs therefore prevents banks from shirking. In summary, a PPP with a well-designed tax-to-subsidy ratio preserves investors' disciplinary role in reducing the bank's moral¬†hazard.
  While we focus on bank runs above, our model has a wide range of applications. In this section, we apply our model to study a market freeze due to coordination failure. Moral hazard in this context is likely to emerge at the individual level. We then show that PPPs are robust to individual moral hazard¬†as¬†well.
  It is widely agreed that coordination failures among financial institutions and investors contributed to the 2008 financial crisis.18Various asset markets suffered from runs and liquidity dry-up, and asset prices fell well below their fundamentals.19The credit market then froze because banks hoard liquidity when anticipating a sluggish real economy.20To illustrate, we focus on the context of a credit market freeze and reinterpret our model. Unlike the benchmark model, the strategic players are nowbanks‚ÄĒinstead of investors‚ÄĒthat individually decide whether to lend to the real economy (ai$a_i$). If enough banks lend (ai=1$a_i=1$), there is sufficient aggregate credit to support the growth of the real economy, and each bank profits from its lending. However, if enough banks abstain from lending (ai=0$a_i=0$), the resulting credit contraction slows economic growth and generates a loss for banks that lend. This gives rise to a so-called self-fulling credit freeze, in which banks withdraw credit supply en masse (Bebchuk and Goldstein (2011)).
  Government guarantees reduce banks' losses from lending and encourage credit supply. However, they also reduce banks' skin in the game and their incentive to screen and monitor borrowers. Recall that in the context of bank runs, the moral hazard problem arises at the aggregate level‚ÄĒif a bank shirks, all of its investors suffer from a lower payoff. In contrast, here, moral hazard emerges at the individual level‚ÄĒif a bank shirks in screening and monitoring, it reduces only that bank's profitability, which does not directly spill over to other¬†banks.
  To incorporate individual moral hazard, we extend our benchmark model to a two-stage game. The first stage is the same as the benchmark model except banks do not receive their payoffs until the second stage. If the realized fundamentall+őł<1$l+\theta <1$in the first stage, then the credit market freezes and all lending banks suffer a net loss normalized to one. If the first-stage realized fundamentall+őł‚Č•1$l+\theta \ge 1$, then aggregate credit supply is sufficient and each lending bank's payoff depends on its individual effort choice in the second stage. If a bank exerts effort to screen and monitor its borrowers, it pays cost of effortce>0$c^e>0$and earns profitR‚ąí1$R-1$with probability 1. Although shirking saves the cost of effort, it reduces banks' profitability. Specifically, if a lending bank shirks, it will suffer a loss of one with probabilityő≥‚Č•0$\gamma \ge 0$and will earn profitR‚ąí1$R-1$with probability1‚ąíő≥$1-\gamma$.
  As in the benchmark model, we consider intervention programs characterized by a subsidy‚Äďtax combination(s,t)$(s,t)$. An important friction here is that the policymaker cannot contract on the effort choice of each individual bank; instead, the tax and subsidy payments can be contingent only on the payoff of each individual bank. In light of this friction, an intervention program stipulates that a participating bank must pay taxtif it profits from lending and will receive subsidysif it suffers a loss due to either a credit market freeze or shirking. In this case, government guarantee programs and PPPs both cost nothing to implement, but the latter are preferable because of their robustness to individual moral¬†hazard.
  We now look for a PPP that can implement the first-best outcome. Furthermore, the ex¬†ante cost of implementing such a PPP also converges¬†to¬†zero.
  
  This proposition demonstrates the advantage of PPPs over government guarantee programs when the moral hazard problem is severe. The subsidy of an intervention program encourages lending in the first stage but deters effort in the second stage. Under a government guarantee program, the policymaker faces a trade-off between first-stage lending efficiency and second-stage effort efficiency. Yet, notwithstanding the moral hazard problem, a PPP still achieves the first-best outcome at zero cost. The advantage of PPPs with respect to individual moral hazard is that they involve only a small mass of participating banks. Although these banks shirk in the second stage, the effect of that shirking on social welfare is limited because the mass of these banks approaches zero as the information friction vanishes. article.IV. Implementation:
Our model provides novel insights for policy design while being agnostic about the actual forms of implementation. In this section, we discuss the practical aspects of implementing PPPs to reduce coordination failure. Following the model, we consider a policymaker as the provider of the intervention program in SectionIV.A. We then examine the viability of privatizing the intervention program in SectionIV.B.
  In the context of panic-driven bank runs as in our main model,21a PPP maps to an insurance program with two features distinct from regular deposit insurance. First, participation isvoluntaryat thedepositorlevel. Second, the price of the insurance induces partial¬†participation.
  In terms of implementation scheme, the most straightforward interpretation is that a policymaker or policymaker-sponsored entity offers such an insurance program directly to depositors. The low cost of implementation implies that the policymaker can downsize the deposit insurance fund (compared with the current one) to back regular deposit insurance. Alternatively, she can decentralize deposit insurance by subsidizing banks that offer such programs to their depositors. To enhance their credibility as an insurer, banks can hold safe and liquid assets‚ÄĒreminiscent of a deposit insurance fund‚ÄĒto back the insurance. Compared with the centralized implementation scheme, a potential drawback is that individual banks are exposed to bank-specific shocks and therefore need to hold a larger amount of safe and liquid assets than does a centralized policymaker to back the insurance¬†program.
  As discussed in SectionIII.B, another type of coordination failure in the financial system is amarket freeze. During the global financial crisis, various asset and credit markets suffered from runs and liquidity dry-up. To support liquidity in asset and credit markets, governments around the world provided financial institutions substantial assistance (e.g., extending access to cheap funding and acquiring illiquid assets). This imposed a heavy fiscal burden on governments and led to moral hazard problems.22In this context, to take advantage of PPPs, policymakers should make the financial assistance unattractive to financial institutions that are optimistic about the recovery and capable of obtaining finance on their¬†own.
  The Capital Purchase Program (CPP) of the Troubled Asset Relief Program (TARP) adopted by the U.S. Treasury can be viewed as an example of a PPP. Under CPP, eligible banks could receive a capital injection from the Treasury in the form of preferred stock or debt securities. In return, participating banks were required to issue warrants to the Treasury enabling it to purchase common shares or other securities in the future. The requirement to issue warrants forced participating banks to share the upside of future recovery with the government and provided strong incentives for optimistic and healthy banks to opt out. Bayazitova and Shivdasani (2012) provide evidence of banks self-selecting into CPP‚ÄĒstrong banks opted out while weak banks¬†participated.
  So far, we consider policymaker-sponsored insurance programs that induce partial participation. In this section, we discuss the viability of the private sector as insurance provider without subsidies from the¬†policymaker.
  We first consider a private insurer as the provider of such an insurance program, whose viability relies on whether the private insurer can profit from doing so. Within our model, the answer is no because as shown in Corollary2, the expected cost of implementing a PPP is always nonnegative. However, a private insurer may find it profitable for reasons outside the model. For example, in the context of a bank run, a bank can benefit from reducing costly default induced by panic runs. Therefore, the bank may have the incentive to self-run the insurance program without subsidies from the policymaker. More broadly, if investors are more risk-averse than a private insurer, there may be gains from trade for the private insurer to provide such an insurance¬†program.
  Although a private insurer may have an incentive to provide the insurance program, we outline two caveats associated with privatized programs. First, investors may face counterparty risk because the insurer must make substantial payments when fundamentals fall slightly below the fundamental threshold. Second, our proposed PPP is an ex ante program, which is independent of realized fundamentals. This requires commitment power from the insurer. A profit-maximizing private insurer has a strong incentive to acquire information about the state of fundamentals and adjust the terms of the insurance program (sandt) accordingly. The public information conveyed by the terms of the insurance program can lead to additional fragility and undermine the efficiency of the insurance program (Angeletos, Hellwig, and Pavan (2006)).
  We next examine the viability of a market-based insurance program, such as the trading of credit default swaps (CDSs) among investors. To fix ideas, consider a self-fulfilling debt crisis in which investors lose confidence in a government and refuse to purchase government debt. This inhibits the government from rolling over its debt, leading to a self-fulfilling default (Cole and Kehoe (2000)). In this context, sovereign CDSs‚ÄĒa form of costly insurance by definition‚ÄĒare similar to our proposed PPPs. Specifically, pessimistic investors can purchase CDSs to reduce the risk associated with government debt, while optimistic investors can directly invest in government debt or even sell CDS protection. This can encourage more investors to purchase government debt and relieve concerns about coordination failures. However, the two caveats about a single private insurer carry over. First, investors may become concerned about counterparty risk, which is likely to increase during a crisis. This reduces the protection provided by CDSs and the role of CDSs in alleviating coordination failure among investors. Second, the price of a CDS can serve as a public signal about investors' aggregate opinion. In particular, a high CDS price reveals overall pessimism about the solvency of the government and discourages investors from purchasing government debt (Shen (2021)).
article.V. Conclusion:
This study analyzes panic-based runs resulting from coordination failures and proposes a novel type of intervention program that policymakers can use to deter runs. The proposed program screens and supports pivotal investors who receive medium signals. Due to strategic complementarities and amplification by higher order beliefs, correctly incentivizing these pivotal investors has a significant effect on all investors. Compared with conventional government guarantee programs, the PPP is not only less costly to implement but also more robust to moral¬†hazard.
  The PPP has a wide range of applications in preventing coordination failures in financial systems. Here, we note its limitations in certain economic environments. First, a PPP requires the policymaker to observe investors' actions and then condition her offer under the program on their run decisions. This may impose additional costs on the policymaker to verify investors' actions. Second, the effectiveness of our proposed program relies on investors being rational. If the rationality of investors is bounded, then the amplification effect (via higher order beliefs) will be¬†limited¬†accordingly.
